Interactions and scaling of regression inputs

Author

Maciej Beręsewicz

1 Introduction

This supplementary notebook covers categorical \(\times\) continuous interactions, centering, standardisation, and Gelman’s (2008) approach to scaling regression inputs. These techniques improve coefficient interpretability, especially in models with interactions.

References:

  • Gelman, A. (2008). Scaling regression inputs by dividing by two standard deviations. Statistics in Medicine, 27(15), 2865–2873. PDF

2 Data

  • R
  • Python
  • Julia
library(ggplot2)
df <- read.csv("../../data/polish-jvs.csv", colClasses = c("character", "factor", rep("character", 4), "numeric"))
nace_counts <- as.data.frame(table(df$nace_division))
names(nace_counts) <- c("nace_division", "n_firms")
df <- merge(df, nace_counts, by = "nace_division")
df$n_firms <- as.numeric(df$n_firms)
head(df[, c("nace_division", "size", "n_firms", "vacancies")])
nace_division size n_firms vacancies
01 Small 923 4
01 Small 923 0
01 Small 923 0
01 Small 923 0
01 Medium 923 0
01 Small 923 0 
import pandas as pd
import numpy as np
import statsmodels.formula.api as smf
import matplotlib.pyplot as plt

df = pd.read_csv("../../data/polish-jvs.csv", dtype={"id": np.int64, "woj":str, "public":str,"size": str, "nace_division": str, "nace": str})
nace_counts = df.groupby("nace_division").size().reset_index(name="n_firms")
df = df.merge(nace_counts, on="nace_division")
print(df[["nace_division", "size", "n_firms", "vacancies"]].head())
  nace_division    size  n_firms  vacancies
0            84   Large     3338          2
1            84   Large     3338          1
2            84   Large     3338          2
3            84  Medium     3338          0
4            84  Medium     3338          0
using CSV, DataFrames, GLM, Statistics

df = CSV.read("../../data/polish-jvs.csv", DataFrame,
              types = Dict(:id => String, :woj=> String, :public=> String,
                           :size => String, :nace_division => String, :nace => String))

3 Categorical \(\times\) continuous interaction

3.1 Model without interaction (parallel lines)

The model vacancies ~ size + n_firms assumes the same slope for n_firms across all size groups – only the intercept differs. The lines are parallel.

  • R
  • Python
  • Julia
model_par <- lm(vacancies ~ size + n_firms, data = df)
summary(model_par)

Call:
lm(formula = vacancies ~ size + n_firms, data = df)

Residuals:
   Min     1Q Median     3Q    Max 
 -2.33  -2.01  -0.53  -0.20 889.68 

Coefficients:
              Estimate Std. Error t value Pr(>|t|)    
(Intercept)  2.328e+00  6.894e-02   33.77   <2e-16 ***
sizeMedium  -1.675e+00  9.577e-02  -17.49   <2e-16 ***
sizeSmall   -1.977e+00  1.038e-01  -19.05   <2e-16 ***
n_firms     -1.332e-04  1.194e-05  -11.15   <2e-16 ***
---
Signif. codes:  0 '***' 0.001 '**' 0.01 '*' 0.05 '.' 0.1 ' ' 1

Residual standard error: 9.744 on 57476 degrees of freedom
Multiple R-squared:  0.01017,   Adjusted R-squared:  0.01012 
F-statistic: 196.8 on 3 and 57476 DF,  p-value: < 2.2e-16
model_par = smf.ols("vacancies ~ size + n_firms", data = df).fit()
print(model_par.summary())

model_par = lm(@formula(vacancies ~ size + n_firms), df)

3.2 Model with interaction (different slopes)

The model vacancies ~ size * n_firms allows a different slope for n_firms in each size group. The coefficient of n_firms is the slope for the reference group (Large). The interaction terms measure how much the slope differs for other groups.

  • R
  • Python
  • Julia
model_slopes <- lm(vacancies ~ size * n_firms, data = df)
summary(model_slopes)

Call:
lm(formula = vacancies ~ size * n_firms, data = df)

Residuals:
   Min     1Q Median     3Q    Max 
 -2.63  -2.08  -0.29  -0.08 889.37 

Coefficients:
                     Estimate Std. Error t value Pr(>|t|)    
(Intercept)         2.635e+00  7.682e-02  34.296  < 2e-16 ***
sizeMedium         -2.326e+00  1.260e-01 -18.455  < 2e-16 ***
sizeSmall          -2.547e+00  1.323e-01 -19.251  < 2e-16 ***
n_firms            -2.330e-04  1.628e-05 -14.311  < 2e-16 ***
sizeMedium:n_firms  2.101e-04  2.643e-05   7.950 1.91e-15 ***
sizeSmall:n_firms   2.289e-04  3.637e-05   6.295 3.10e-10 ***
---
Signif. codes:  0 '***' 0.001 '**' 0.01 '*' 0.05 '.' 0.1 ' ' 1

Residual standard error: 9.737 on 57474 degrees of freedom
Multiple R-squared:  0.01157,   Adjusted R-squared:  0.01148 
F-statistic: 134.6 on 5 and 57474 DF,  p-value: < 2.2e-16
## Slopes by group
b <- coef(model_slopes)
cat("Slope for Large:", b["n_firms"], "\n")
Slope for Large: -0.0002329657 
cat("Slope for Medium:", b["n_firms"] + b["sizeMedium:n_firms"], "\n")
Slope for Medium: -2.282689e-05 
cat("Slope for Small:", b["n_firms"] + b["sizeSmall:n_firms"], "\n")
Slope for Small: -4.025062e-06 
model_slopes = smf.ols("vacancies ~ size * n_firms", data = df).fit()
print(model_slopes.summary())
                            OLS Regression Results

## Slopes by group
b = model_slopes.params
print(f"Slope for Large: {b['n_firms']:.6f}")
Slope for Large: -0.000233
print(f"Slope for Medium: {b['n_firms'] + b['size[T.Medium]:n_firms']:.6f}")
Slope for Medium: -0.000023
print(f"Slope for Small: {b['n_firms'] + b['size[T.Small]:n_firms']:.6f}")
Slope for Small: -0.000004
model_slopes = lm(@formula(vacancies ~ size * n_firms), df)

vacancies ~ 1 + size + n_firms + size & n_firms

──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
(Intercept)              2.63465      0.0768213    34.30    <1e-99   2.48408       2.78522

──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────

## Slopes by group
b = coef(model_slopes)
6-element Vector{Float64}:
  2.634651667085122
 -2.3255286877484513
 -2.546939698404122
 -0.00023296573351904064
  0.00021013884309311294
  0.00022894067132381453
println("Slope for Large: ", b[4])
Slope for Large: -0.00023296573351904064

3.3 Visualising parallel vs. crossing lines

  • R
  • Python
df$fitted_par <- fitted(model_par)
df$fitted_int <- fitted(model_slopes)

## Use distinct n_firms values per size group for clean lines
plot_data <- aggregate(cbind(fitted_par, fitted_int) ~ n_firms + size, data = df, FUN = mean)

p1 <- ggplot(plot_data, aes(x = n_firms, y = fitted_par, color = size)) +
  geom_line(linewidth = 1) +
  labs(title = "Without interaction (parallel lines)",
       y = "Fitted vacancies", x = "Number of firms in sector") +
  theme_minimal()

p2 <- ggplot(plot_data, aes(x = n_firms, y = fitted_int, color = size)) +
  geom_line(linewidth = 1) +
  labs(title = "With interaction (different slopes)",
       y = "Fitted vacancies", x = "Number of firms in sector") +
  theme_minimal()

library(patchwork)
p1 + p2
df["fitted_par"] = model_par.fittedvalues
df["fitted_int"] = model_slopes.fittedvalues

plot_data = df.groupby(["n_firms", "size"])[["fitted_par", "fitted_int"]].mean().reset_index()

fig, axes = plt.subplots(1, 2, figsize=(12, 4))
for name, grp in plot_data.groupby("size"):
    axes[0].plot(grp["n_firms"], grp["fitted_par"], label=name)
    axes[1].plot(grp["n_firms"], grp["fitted_int"], label=name)
axes[0].set_title("Without interaction (parallel lines)")
axes[1].set_title("With interaction (different slopes)")
for ax in axes:
    ax.set_xlabel("Number of firms in sector")
    ax.set_ylabel("Fitted vacancies")
    ax.legend()
plt.tight_layout()
plt.show()

4 Centering continuous variables

4.1 Why center?

In a model with interaction vacancies ~ size * n_firms, the “main effect” of size is the effect when n_firms = 0. But n_firms = 0 is outside the observed range (there are no sectors with zero firms), so this main effect is an extrapolation with no practical meaning.

Grand-mean centering replaces n_firms with n_firms - mean(n_firms). Now the main effect of size is the effect at the average sector size, which is much more interpretable.

4.2 Grand-mean centering

  • R
  • Python
  • Julia
df$n_firms_c <- df$n_firms - mean(df$n_firms)

model_cent <- lm(vacancies ~ size * n_firms_c, data = df)
summary(model_cent)

Call:
lm(formula = vacancies ~ size * n_firms_c, data = df)

Residuals:
   Min     1Q Median     3Q    Max 
 -2.63  -2.08  -0.29  -0.08 889.37 

Coefficients:
                       Estimate Std. Error t value Pr(>|t|)    
(Intercept)           1.971e+00  5.847e-02  33.713  < 2e-16 ***
sizeMedium           -1.727e+00  9.593e-02 -18.002  < 2e-16 ***
sizeSmall            -1.895e+00  1.064e-01 -17.811  < 2e-16 ***
n_firms_c            -2.330e-04  1.628e-05 -14.311  < 2e-16 ***
sizeMedium:n_firms_c  2.101e-04  2.643e-05   7.950 1.91e-15 ***
sizeSmall:n_firms_c   2.289e-04  3.637e-05   6.295 3.10e-10 ***
---
Signif. codes:  0 '***' 0.001 '**' 0.01 '*' 0.05 '.' 0.1 ' ' 1

Residual standard error: 9.737 on 57474 degrees of freedom
Multiple R-squared:  0.01157,   Adjusted R-squared:  0.01148 
F-statistic: 134.6 on 5 and 57474 DF,  p-value: < 2.2e-16
df["n_firms_c"] = df["n_firms"] - df["n_firms"].mean()

model_cent = smf.ols("vacancies ~ size * n_firms_c", data = df).fit()
print(model_cent.summary())
                            OLS Regression Results

StatsModels.TableRegressionModel{LinearModel{GLM.LmResp{Vector{Float64}}, GLM.DensePredChol{Float64, CholeskyPivoted{Float64, Matrix{Float64}, Vector{Int64}}}}, Matrix{Float64}}

vacancies ~ 1 + size + n_firms_c + size & n_firms_c

4.3 What changes and what stays the same?

  • R
  • Python
  • Julia
## R-squared: identical
cat("R² (uncentered):", summary(model_slopes)$r.squared, "\n")
R<U+00B2> (uncentered): 0.01157001 
cat("R² (centered):  ", summary(model_cent)$r.squared, "\n")
R<U+00B2> (centered):   0.01157001 
## Interaction coefficients: identical
cat("\nInteraction coefs (uncentered):\n")

Interaction coefs (uncentered):
print(coef(model_slopes)[5:6])
sizeMedium:n_firms  sizeSmall:n_firms 
      0.0002101388       0.0002289407 
cat("Interaction coefs (centered):\n")
Interaction coefs (centered):
print(coef(model_cent)[5:6])
sizeMedium:n_firms_c  sizeSmall:n_firms_c 
        0.0002101388         0.0002289407 
## Main-effect coefficients: different
cat("\nMain effects (uncentered):\n")

Main effects (uncentered):
print(coef(model_slopes)[1:3])
(Intercept)  sizeMedium   sizeSmall 
   2.634652   -2.325529   -2.546940 
cat("Main effects (centered):\n")
Main effects (centered):
print(coef(model_cent)[1:3])
(Intercept)  sizeMedium   sizeSmall 
   1.971168   -1.727056   -1.894919 
## Fitted values: identical
cat("\nFitted values identical?", all.equal(fitted(model_slopes), fitted(model_cent)), "\n")

Fitted values identical? TRUE 
## R-squared: identical
print(f"R² (uncentered): {model_slopes.rsquared:.6f}")
R² (uncentered): 0.011570
print(f"R² (centered):   {model_cent.rsquared:.6f}")
R² (centered):   0.011570
## Interaction coefficients: identical
print(f"\nInteraction coefs (uncentered):")

Interaction coefs (uncentered):
print(model_slopes.params.filter(like=":"))
size[T.Medium]:n_firms    0.000210
size[T.Small]:n_firms     0.000229

4.4 Standardisation (z-score)

Standardising (z-score) additionally rescales to standard deviation units, so coefficients become comparable across variables with different scales.

  • R
  • Python
  • Julia
df$n_firms_s <- as.numeric(scale(df$n_firms))

model_std <- lm(vacancies ~ size * n_firms_s, data = df)
summary(model_std)

Call:
lm(formula = vacancies ~ size * n_firms_s, data = df)

df["n_firms_s"] = (df["n_firms"] - df["n_firms"].mean()) / df["n_firms"].std()

model_std = smf.ols("vacancies ~ size * n_firms_s", data = df).fit()
print(model_std.summary())

5 Gelman’s approach: scaling by two standard deviations

5.1 The problem with standard standardisation

When comparing continuous and binary predictors in the same model, dividing the continuous input by 1 SD does not make coefficients directly comparable:

  • A binary (0/1) variable like public has a natural 1-unit change (from 0 to 1), representing its full range.
  • For a continuous variable, a 1-SD change is only a moderate shift – roughly covering 68% of the distribution.

Gelman (2008) proposes a simple fix: divide continuous inputs by 2 SD instead of 1 SD. Then a 1-unit change in the rescaled continuous variable corresponds to moving from 1 SD below to 1 SD above the mean – a “typical” swing that is more comparable in magnitude to the 0-to-1 swing of a binary predictor.

5.2 Centering binary variables by their means

Gelman also recommends centering binary inputs by their means. Instead of leaving public as 0/1 with the intercept representing the public = 0 group:

  • Replace public with public - mean(public)
  • The intercept now represents the expected value at the “average” mix of public/private firms, not just at the reference level

This is especially useful in models with interactions, where the “main effect” of a categorical variable is conditional on the other variable being at its reference level.

5.3 Implementation

  • R
  • Python
  • Julia
## Gelman scaling: continuous by 2*SD
df$n_firms_g <- (df$n_firms - mean(df$n_firms)) / (2 * sd(df$n_firms))

## Center binary by its mean
df$public_num <- as.numeric(df$public == "1")
df$public_c <- df$public_num - mean(df$public_num)

## Model with Gelman-scaled inputs
model_gelman <- lm(vacancies ~ size + n_firms_g + public_c, data = df)
summary(model_gelman)

Call:
lm(formula = vacancies ~ size + n_firms_g + public_c, data = df)

Residuals:
   Min     1Q Median     3Q    Max 
 -2.39  -2.00  -0.57  -0.10 889.90 

Coefficients:
            Estimate Std. Error t value Pr(>|t|)    
(Intercept)  1.96738    0.05879  33.467  < 2e-16 ***
sizeMedium  -1.69803    0.09609 -17.672  < 2e-16 ***
sizeSmall   -2.02823    0.10526 -19.270  < 2e-16 ***
n_firms_g   -0.77898    0.09411  -8.277  < 2e-16 ***
public_c    -0.29187    0.09972  -2.927  0.00343 ** 
---
Signif. codes:  0 '***' 0.001 '**' 0.01 '*' 0.05 '.' 0.1 ' ' 1

Residual standard error: 9.744 on 57475 degrees of freedom
Multiple R-squared:  0.01032,   Adjusted R-squared:  0.01025 
F-statistic: 149.8 on 4 and 57475 DF,  p-value: < 2.2e-16
## Gelman scaling
df["n_firms_g"] = (df["n_firms"] - df["n_firms"].mean()) / (2 * df["n_firms"].std())

## Center binary by its mean
df["public_num"] = (df["public"] == "1").astype(float)
df["public_c"] = df["public_num"] - df["public_num"].mean()

model_gelman = smf.ols("vacancies ~ size + n_firms_g + public_c", data=df).fit()
print(model_gelman.summary())

5.4 Comparison: coefficient magnitudes

Compare the same model under three scaling approaches to see how coefficient comparability changes.

  • R
  • Python
  • Julia
## Original (no scaling)
model_orig <- lm(vacancies ~ size + n_firms + public_num, data = df)

## Standard z-score (1 SD)
model_1sd <- lm(vacancies ~ size + n_firms_s + public_num, data = df)

## Gelman (2 SD for continuous, mean-centered binary)
model_2sd <- lm(vacancies ~ size + n_firms_g + public_c, data = df)

comparison <- data.frame(
  original = coef(model_orig),
  z_score_1sd = coef(model_1sd),
  gelman_2sd = coef(model_2sd)
)
round(comparison, 4)
original z_score_1sd gelman_2sd
(Intercept) 2.3934 2.0702 1.9674
sizeMedium -1.6980 -1.6980 -1.6980
sizeSmall -2.0282 -2.0282 -2.0282
n_firms -0.0001 -0.3895 -0.7790
public_num -0.2919 -0.2919 -0.2919

Note how the Gelman-scaled coefficient for n_firms_g and public_c are now on a more comparable scale: both represent the effect of a “typical” change in the predictor.

model_orig = smf.ols("vacancies ~ size + n_firms + public_num", data=df).fit()
model_1sd = smf.ols("vacancies ~ size + n_firms_s + public_num", data=df).fit()
model_2sd = smf.ols("vacancies ~ size + n_firms_g + public_c", data=df).fit()

comparison = pd.DataFrame({
    "original": model_orig.params,
    "z_score_1sd": model_1sd.params,
    "gelman_2sd": model_2sd.params
})
print(comparison.round(4))
                original  z_score_1sd  gelman_2sd

n_firms_g            NaN          NaN     -0.7790
n_firms_s            NaN      -0.3895         NaN
public_c             NaN          NaN     -0.2919
public_num       -0.2919      -0.2919         NaN
size[T.Medium]   -1.6980      -1.6980     -1.6980
size[T.Small]    -2.0282      -2.0282     -2.0282
model_orig = lm(@formula(vacancies ~ size + n_firms + public_num), df)
